sources.notes: Statement and constraints: compute the expected number of unordered pairs of vertices that become leaves after independently falling vertices are removed from a tree.
Official editorial partitions vertex pairs into adjacent, distance two via a shared neighbor, and all other pairs, then computes/corrects their probability contributions.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu candidate source; not used in this review because the official editorial was sufficient.

annotation.summary: Given independent falling probabilities on tree vertices, compute the expected number of unordered pairs of distinct vertices that are leaves in the remaining forest.
annotation.constraints: Total n over tests is at most 1e5; probabilities are modular fractions, so all pair contributions must be summarized in linear or near-linear time.
annotation.core_idea: Use linearity of expectation over unordered vertex pairs. First compute for each vertex the probability p2[i] that it becomes a leaf. Summing p2[u] * p2[v] over all pairs is correct only when the local neighborhoods of u and v are independent, which fails for adjacent pairs and pairs sharing a neighbor. Recompute those two local categories explicitly and replace their naive contributions.
annotation.complexity: O(n log M) per test batch, dominated by modular inverses; the tree traversal and pair corrections are linear in total degree.
tricks: Classify unordered pairs by tree distance: adjacent, distance two, or independent farther pairs.
Start with the all-pairs product of individual leaf probabilities, then subtract and replace local dependent cases.
Use products over neighbors and divide by one neighbor's probability to derive per-edge or shared-neighbor contributions.

solution_variants.name: local-dependency-expectation
solution_variants.summary: Compute each vertex's base leaf probability, sum all pair products, and correct pairs at distance one or two using formulas that condition on their shared local neighborhood.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log M) time for modular inverses and O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: The answer is the sum over unordered pairs of the probability that both endpoints become leaves.
The solution's decisive split is adjacent pairs, pairs sharing a neighbor, and all remaining pairs.
tags.description: Count, enumerate, or correct vertex pairs whose graph distance is at most two, often separating adjacent pairs and common-neighbor pairs from all far pairs.
tags.created_reason: Existing graph/tree tags do not capture the recurring local-pair classification by distance one and two.

tags.evidence: The distance-two pair category has a unique shared neighbor because the input graph is a tree.
The implementation starts from all pair contributions and locally replaces the contributions of dependent pairs.
All formulas manipulate independent falling probabilities and modular inverse probabilities.
